title: Getting started with modelblueprint
text: modelblueprint S7 object wraps fitted model alongside training data, pipeline functions, deployment metadata. goal single object carries everything needed reproduce predictions, run diagnostics, save/restore model. example fits Poisson frequency model small synthetic car insurance dataset. claim_freq (claims per unit exposure) target; nclaims raw count modelled log-exposure offset.
code: # Synthetic car insurance data ------------------------------------------------ set.seed(42L) n   <- 500L ins <- data.frame(   driver_age  = sample(18L:75L, n, replace = TRUE),   vehicle_age = sample(0L:15L,  n, replace = TRUE),   gender      = sample(c("M", "F"), n, replace = TRUE),   exposure    = round(runif(n, 0.1, 1.0), 2) ) ins$nclaims    <- rpois(n, lambda = ins$exposure * exp(   log(0.08) + ifelse(ins$driver_age < 25, 0.5, 0) + 0.01 * ins$vehicle_age )) ins$claim_freq <- ins$nclaims / ins$exposure train <- ins[1:400, ] test  <- ins[401:500, ]  # Wrap the fitted model ------------------------------------------------------- mb <- modelblueprint(   model = glm(     nclaims ~ driver_age + vehicle_age + gender + offset(log(exposure)),     data   = train,     family = poisson   ),   post_process_fun   = function(preds, df_raw) preds / df_raw$exposure,   train              = train,   test               = test,   y_name             = "claim_freq",   expo_name          = "exposure",   x_original_inputs  = c("driver_age", "vehicle_age", "gender"),   model_display_name = "glm_poisson_freq",   deploy_notes       = "GLM Poisson frequency: motor third-party liability" )  print(mb) #> ============================================================  #> modelblueprint #> ============================================================  #>   Model:        glm/lm #>   Display name: glm_poisson_freq #>   Target:       claim_freq #>   Exposure:     exposure (val = 1) #>   Features:     3 original / 0 engineered #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>   Train rows:   400 #>   Test rows:    100 #>   Holdout rows: <not set> #> ------------------------------------------------------------  #>   Notes: GLM Poisson frequency: motor third-party liability #> ============================================================

text: Use feat_eng_fun apply transformations prediction — function applied training data new data prediction time.
code: mb_fe <- modelblueprint(   model = glm(     nclaims ~ driver_age + driver_age2 + vehicle_age + offset(log(exposure)),     data   = transform(train, driver_age2 = driver_age^2),     family = poisson   ),   feat_eng_fun       = function(df) transform(df, driver_age2 = driver_age^2),   post_process_fun   = function(preds, df_raw) preds / df_raw$exposure,   train              = train,   y_name             = "claim_freq",   expo_name          = "exposure" )  head(predict(mb_fe, test)) #> [1] 0.07629813 0.12936442 0.14925420 0.07331023 0.17461602 0.11211892

what: Large models and the prediction cache
title: Interactive model dashboard
text: H2O, XGBoost, model single predict() call full dataset takes several seconds, dashboard unusably slow chart computed predictions independently. gain, predicted-vs-observed, residuals, distribution, one-way overlay charts trigger separate full-dataset scoring pass. mb_dashboard() avoids per-set prediction cache: first chart needs predictions given split computes stores result; every subsequent chart session reads cache. notification appears computation runs UI appear frozen. precomputed_preds argument available every individual diagnostic function, can take advantage cache working outside dashboard :
code: # The cache is transparent --- launch the dashboard as normal. # For a large H2O model: mb_dashboard(mb_h2o_glm_large(n = 50000L)) preds <- predict(mb, mb@train)  gain(mb,               set = "train", precomputed_preds = preds) pred_vs_obs(mb,        set = "train", precomputed_preds = preds) residuals_grouped(mb,  set = "train", precomputed_preds = preds) one_way(mb, var = "driver_age", predictions = TRUE, precomputed_preds = preds)

title: Model diagnostics
text: modelblueprint provides four diagnostic charts evaluating model performance. works plain data frame dispatches automatically modelblueprint object.

title: Model diagnostics
text: gains chart measures well model rank-orders risk. Predictions sorted highest lowest, chart shows proportion total observed losses captured move portfolio. perfect model captures losses top-risk segment. random model follows diagonal. Gini coefficient summarises rank-ordering ability single number -1 1 — higher better. use : gains chart primary performance metric insurance pricing. Gini 0.3 generally considered useful pricing; 0.5 strong.
code: # Gains chart — Gini shown in legend gain(mb)

title: Model diagnostics
text: calibration chart bins predictions plots average predicted rate average observed rate per bin. well-calibrated model points close diagonal — predicted observed equal average. chart three elements: Yellow bars — exposure per bin (right axis) Blue dots — observed rate per bin Dashed black line — predicted rate per bin dots track dashed line closely, model well-calibrated. Systematic divergence tails indicates model underestimates overestimates extreme risks. use : Use fitting check predicted rates match observed rates across full prediction range. Also useful comparing recalibrated vs uncalibrated model.
code: # Calibration chart pred_vs_obs(mb) # More bins for finer resolution pred_vs_obs(mb, bins = 20L)  # Equal-range bins (useful when predictions are skewed) pred_vs_obs(mb, type_agg = "equal_range")

title: Model diagnostics
text: residuals chart bins predictions exposure plots grouped residuals — difference observed predicted — loess trend line 95% confidence interval. well-specified model residuals scattered randomly around zero. systematic pattern indicates model misspecification. exposure_per_bin argument controls many data points go group — smaller values give groups noisier detailed view. use : Use diagnose model systematically - -predicts. upward trend right tail means model underestimates high-risk predictions; downward trend means overestimates.
code: residuals_grouped(mb) # Pearson residuals — scaled by sqrt(pred), useful for count/rate models residuals_grouped(mb, residual_type = "pearson")  # Control grouping granularity via exposure per bin residuals_grouped(mb, exposure_per_bin = 500)

title: Model diagnostics
text: SAMI chart compares two competing models binning ratio one model’s predictions another. bin ratio, shows observed mean alongside model predictions. ratio far 1, models disagree substantially. observed line reveals model closer reality region. plain data frame predictions already attached: recalib = TRUE, predictions scaled match observed mean computing ratios — isolates shape differences overall level differences. use : SAMI useful deciding two candidate models. shows just model performs better overall, one model better much. regions observed line tracks one model closely drive decision.
code: # Compare two frequency blueprints mb1 <- mb_glm_poisson_freq() mb2 <- mb_lm_regression()   # different model on different data — illustrative  sami(list(mb1, mb2), bins = 10L) train         <- mb@train train$pred1   <- predict(mb, train) train$pred2   <- predict(mb, train) * runif(nrow(train), 0.8, 1.2)  # perturbed  sami(train, obs = "claim_freq", pred = c("pred1", "pred2"),      exposure = "exposure", bins = 10) sami(list(mb1, mb2), bins = 10L, recalib = TRUE)

what: What is a partial dependence plot?
title: Partial dependence plots
text: partial dependence plot (PDP) reveals marginal effect single feature model output, averaging influence features. Unlike one-way plot — shows raw observed predicted means per bin — PDP fixes feature series values averages predictions across full training distribution. makes PDPs especially useful : Understanding non-linear feature effects black-box models Comparing shape relationship across competing models Diagnosing interactions unexpected behaviour chart dual-axis layout one-way plots: Yellow bars (right axis) — exposure per bin Three lines (left axis) — observed mean, -sample predicted mean, PDP (marginal effect)

title: Partial dependence plots
text: plain data frame:
code: # PDP for driver_age — everything pulled from blueprint slots pdp(mb, var = "driver_age") #> ℹ Calculating pdp for `driver_age` model <- glm(   nclaims ~ driver_age + vehicle_age + offset(log(exposure)),   data = mb@train, family = poisson ) pdp(mb@train, var = "driver_age", obs = "claim_freq", model = model)

title: Partial dependence plots
text: PDP chart shows three lines per feature: Observed — exposure-weighted mean actual target per bin. one-way plot. Predicted (-sample) — exposure-weighted mean predict(model, data) per bin. Tracks observed line model well-calibrated. PDP — true marginal effect. bin midpoint, feature fixed value across rows training data predictions averaged. isolates feature’s contribution correlations features. PDP line diverges predicted line, signals feature’s -sample predictions driven partly correlations rather feature .

title: Partial dependence plots
text: PDP computation requires predicting across full dataset bin. large datasets, sample_size caps number rows used: default 10,000. models, 1,000 rows gives stable PDP line fraction computation cost.
code: # Coarser view pdp(mb, var = "driver_age", bins = 8L) #> ℹ Calculating pdp for `driver_age` # Use at most 1,000 rows for PDP computation pdp(mb, var = "vehicle_value", sample_size = 1000L) #> ℹ Calculating pdp for `vehicle_value`

what: modelblueprint: Model Diagnostics and Explainability for Machine Learning Workflows — modelblueprint-package
title: modelblueprint: Model Diagnostics and Explainability for Machine Learning Workflows — modelblueprint-package
text: model-agnostic toolkit exploring, diagnosing, deploying machine learning models. Wraps fitted model (lm, glm, XGBoost, H2O, random forests, ) structured S7 object carries training data, feature engineering pipelines, deployment metadata. Provides one-way analysis plots, partial dependence plots (PDPs), SHAP feature importance, cumulative gains charts, calibration plots, grouped residual diagnostics — rendered interactive Plotly charts. Supports sequential model pipelines via mb_seq() saves complete model bundles (including H2O XGBoost models) portable archives. Aggregation handled data.table scales comfortably datasets several million rows.

title: Permutation-based SHAP value computation — compute_shap
text: feature j vars observation , runs nsim random permutations vars feature ordering. permutation s: vars features appearing j ("coalition") keep actual values; vars features j swapped background row without_j, with_j keeps j actual swaps features j. Averaging permutations gives unbiased Shapley value estimate.

title: SHAP Feature Importance and Dependence Plots — shap
code: shap(data, ...)  # Default S3 method shap(   data,   model,   vars,   exposure = "exposure",   type = c("importance", "dependence"),   nsim = 50L,   sample_size = 500L,   bins = 10L,   type_agg = c("equal_exposure", "equal_range"),   ret = c("plot", "data"),   model_name = "model",   pre_process_fun = function(df) df,   feat_eng_fun = function(df) df,   post_process_fun = function(preds, df_raw) preds,   seed = 2024L,   ... )

title: SHAP Feature Importance and Dependence Plots — shap
text: data data.frame data.table. Must contain columns named vars , optionally, exposure. ... Unused. model fitted model object. vars [character] Feature column names SHAP values computed. exposure [character(1)] Exposure weight column. column absent, every row given weight 1. Default "exposure". Used dependence plot (exposure bars weighted mean SHAP line). type [character(1)] Plot type: "importance" (default) returns signed horizontal bar chart mean SHAP per feature (purple = positive effect, blue = negative effect, sorted |SHAP| magnitude); "dependence" returns dual-axis chart (exposure bars + mean SHAP per bin) variable vars. nsim [integer(1)] Number random permutations per observation. Higher values give stable SHAP estimates cost compute time. Default 50L. sample_size [integer(1)] Rows sampled data SHAP computation. Default 500L. Seeded 2024 reproducibility. bins [integer(1)] Number bins dependence plot x-axis. Default 10L. type_agg [character(1)] Binning strategy dependence plot: "equal_exposure" (default) "equal_range". ret [character(1)] "plot" (default) "data". "data" returns data.table one SHAP column per feature vars. model_name [character(1)] Label shown plot titles. Default "model". pre_process_fun function(df) -> df applied feat_eng_fun. Default identity function. feat_eng_fun function(df) -> df (matrix) produces model input. Default identity function. post_process_fun function(preds, df_raw) -> numeric applied raw model predictions. Default identity function. seed [integer(1)] Seed row sample internal SHAP permutations, applied via withr::with_seed() global RNG stream left undisturbed results reproducible. Default 2024L.
